planting.note: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost or start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; lemon balm germinates readily in cool soil and grows vigorously in full sun to partial shade, tolerating more shade than most herbs.

directions: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow outdoors 4–6 weeks before last frost in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; lemon balm tolerates more shade than most herbs and is one of the few culinary herbs that produces acceptable quality even in partially shaded sites. Melissa officinalis is a vigorous, lemon-scented European perennial in the mint family producing wrinkled, oval, toothed leaves with a delightful fresh lemon fragrance used in teas, lemonade, salads, fish dishes, poultry, desserts, and liqueurs; it is much milder and more pleasant than lemon verbena for fresh use. Cut plants back hard after flowering to prevent aggressive self-seeding and to stimulate a flush of fresh, fragrant new leaves; in the Gulf South it often dies back in summer heat but re-emerges in fall. Harvest before flowering for best fragrance and flavor.

planting.note: Sow direct or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; oregano requires excellent drainage and lean, infertile soil for the strongest flavor — rich soil produces rank growth with weak aroma.

directions: Sow direct after last frost or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; avoid rich, fertile soil which produces lush but relatively flavorless plants — oregano's essential oils are most concentrated when the plant is slightly stressed by poor soil and minimal water. Origanum vulgare is the common wild oregano of Europe and the source of dried Italian-style oregano; the species varies enormously in flavor intensity depending on climate and selection, with Mediterranean-climate plants (especially Greek 'Hirtum') being the most pungent, while cool-climate plants can be nearly flavorless. The species naturalizes easily in dry, sunny banks and rock gardens and produces masses of tiny, pink to white flowers in summer that are excellent nectar sources for bees and butterflies. Subsp. hirtum (Greek oregano) is the pungent, strongly flavored selection recommended for culinary use.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; sweet marjoram is frost-tender and should only be transplanted outdoors after all danger of frost has passed — seeds are tiny and germinate erratically indoors at 65–70°F.

directions: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost date in well-drained, slightly alkaline seed mix; seeds are tiny and slow to germinate — do not cover deeply. Transplant outdoors after all frost risk has passed in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun, spacing 9–12 inches apart; sweet marjoram is frost-tender and is grown as an annual in all but the mildest climates. Origanum majorana is the sweeter, more delicate culinary counterpart to wild oregano, with a complex, warm, floral-herbal flavor that is the defining ingredient in many European sausage and stuffing recipes, and is used extensively in French and German cooking where oregano would be too pungent; the flavor diminishes sharply after cooking, so add at the end. Harvest branch tips frequently before flowering to keep plants producing fresh foliage through summer; in zones 9–11 it persists as a perennial shrublet.

planting.note: Sow direct around last frost date in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; winter savory requires perfect drainage and is excellent in herb gardens, rock gardens, and container plantings.

directions: Sow direct around last frost date in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; winter savory requires excellent drainage and performs poorly in heavy clay or moist soil. Satureja montana is a woody-based, semi-evergreen perennial herb with narrow, glossy, dark-green, very aromatic leaves and masses of tiny, lilac-white flowers in late summer; it has a more robust, resinous, slightly bitter-peppery flavor compared to the more delicate annual summer savory (S. hortensis) and is the traditional seasoning for beans and lentils in many European countries, particularly in the south of France (where it is called poivre d'âne — donkey pepper). The semi-evergreen foliage can be harvested year-round in mild climates; it is an attractive, low, mounded, shrubby herb for the front of sunny borders and herb gardens.

planting.note: Sow direct after last frost in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; summer savory is a fast-growing annual that can be succession-sown every 3–4 weeks for a continuous harvest.

directions: Sow direct after last frost in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; summer savory is a fast-growing annual that can be direct sown every 3–4 weeks for a continuous harvest through the growing season. Satureja hortensis is a traditional European culinary herb with a long history of cultivation; it has a bright, thyme-like flavor with peppery notes — milder and more delicate than winter savory — and is traditionally used to season green beans (the German name Bohnenkraut means "bean herb"), sausages, stuffings, soups, and roasted meats. It is one of the essential components of the French herbes de Provence blend alongside thyme, rosemary, oregano, and lavender. The plant produces attractive, lavender-pink flowers loved by bees and is useful as an edging plant in the potager.

planting.note: Sow seed direct in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun, or plant divisions or cuttings in spring or fall; wild thyme is excellent as a lawn substitute, ground cover between stepping stones, and in rock gardens.

directions: Sow seed in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; wild thyme is variable from seed — for specific flower color or mat habit, use vegetatively propagated plants. Thymus serpyllum is the low, mat-forming wild thyme of European heaths and rocky hillsides, spreading to form dense, fragrant carpets 2–6 inches tall bearing tiny, deep rose-pink to purple flowers in early summer that are covered with honeybees and butterflies; it is equally valuable as a culinary herb (leaves have a pleasant, mild thyme flavor) and as a ground cover, lawn substitute, and rock garden plant. It tolerates light foot traffic, making it excellent for planting between stepping stones and in paths; it is more cold-hardy and mat-forming than common thyme (T. vulgaris) and significantly more ornamental in flower. Shear lightly after blooming to keep compact.
